examples.title: Algorithm: RDD Transformations and Actions
examples.description: Demonstrates how Spark uses lazy transformations and eager actions to process distributed data through RDDs
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates Spark's lazy evaluation model. Transformations (filter, map, reduceByKey) build a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) without executing. Only when an action (collect, count, reduce) is called does Spark execute the entire pipeline. RDDs maintain lineage information for fault tolerance - if a partition is lost, Spark recomputes it from the original data.

examples.title: Python: Word Count with RDDs
examples.description: Implements a classic distributed word count problem using Spark RDD transformations and actions
examples.code:
from pyspark import SparkContext, SparkConf
import re

# Initialize Spark Context
conf = SparkConf()\
    .setAppName("WordCountApp")\
    .setMaster("local[*]")  # Use all available cores

sc = SparkContext(conf=conf)

# Method 1: Create RDD from local collection
data = ["hello world", "hello spark", "world of big data"]
rdd = sc.parallelize(data)

# Method 2: Create RDD from text file (uncomment to use)
# rdd = sc.textFile("input.txt", minPartitions=4)

# TRANSFORMATIONS (Lazy - build DAG)
# FlatMap: Split each line into words
words_rdd = rdd.flatMap(lambda line: line.split())

# Map: Create (word, 1) pairs
pairs_rdd = words_rdd.map(lambda word: (word.lower(), 1))

# ReduceByKey: Aggregate counts for each word
# This is a "wide transformation" - requires shuffling
word_counts = pairs_rdd.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)

# Sort by count (another transformation)
sorted_counts = word_counts.sortBy(lambda x: x[1], ascending=False)

# ACTION: Trigger execution
# collect() returns all elements to driver
results = sorted_counts.collect()

# Print results
print("\n=== Word Count Results ===")
for word, count in results:
    print(f"{word}: {count}")

# Other useful ACTIONS:
total_words = words_rdd.count()
print(f"\nTotal words: {total_words}")

first_three = sorted_counts.take(3)
print(f"\nTop 3 words: {first_three}")

# CACHE for reuse (persist in memory)
words_rdd.cache()

# Example: Filter and process cached data
filtered = words_rdd.filter(lambda w: len(w) > 4)
print(f"\nWords with more than 4 letters: {filtered.count()}")

# Stop SparkContext
sc.stop()

# Time Complexity: O(n) where n is total words
# Space Complexity: O(k) where k is unique words
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the core RDD operations in PySpark. The flatMap transformation splits each line into individual words, map creates key-value pairs, and reduceByKey aggregates the counts. All transformations are lazy - they build the execution plan but don't run until collect() is called. The shuffle operation in reduceByKey creates stage boundaries in the DAG.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for processing n words, O(k log k) for sorting k unique words
examples.spaceComplexity: O(k) for storing k unique word counts in memory

examples.title: Python: DataFrame Operations with Spark SQL
examples.description: Demonstrates structured data processing using Spark DataFrames and SQL queries for IoT sensor data
examples.code:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, avg, max, min, count, when, window
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, DoubleType, TimestampType
import datetime

# Create SparkSession (entry point to Spark SQL)
spark = SparkSession.builder\
    .appName("IoTSensorAnalytics")\
    .master("local[*]")\
    .getOrCreate()

# Define schema for IoT sensor data
schema = StructType([
    StructField("sensor_id", StringType(), True),
    StructField("temperature", DoubleType(), True),
    StructField("humidity", DoubleType(), True),
    StructField("timestamp", TimestampType(), True),
    StructField("location", StringType(), True)
])

# Create sample IoT sensor data
sensor_data = [
    ("sensor_01", 25.5, 60.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 0), "room_a"),
    ("sensor_01", 26.0, 58.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 5), "room_a"),
    ("sensor_01", 27.2, 55.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 10), "room_a"),
    ("sensor_02", 22.0, 70.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 0), "room_b"),
    ("sensor_02", 23.5, 68.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 5), "room_b"),
    ("sensor_02", 24.0, 65.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 10), "room_b"),
    ("sensor_01", 28.0, 52.0, datetime.datetime(2024, 1, 1, 10, 15), "room_a"),
]

# Create DataFrame
df = spark.createDataFrame(sensor_data, schema)

# Print schema
print("=== DataFrame Schema ===")
df.printSchema()

# Show all data
print("\n=== All Sensor Data ===")
df.show()

# DataFrame Operations (similar to pandas but distributed)
print("\n=== Temperature > 25°C ===")
high_temp = df.filter(col("temperature") > 25)
high_temp.show()

# Aggregation by sensor
print("\n=== Aggregations by Sensor ===")
sensor_stats = df.groupBy("sensor_id").agg(
    count("*").alias("reading_count"),
    avg("temperature").alias("avg_temperature"),
    max("temperature").alias("max_temperature"),
    min("temperature").alias("min_temperature"),
    avg("humidity").alias("avg_humidity")
)
sensor_stats.show()

# Conditional column creation
print("\n=== With Alert Status ===")
df_with_alerts = df.withColumn(
    "alert",
    when(col("temperature") > 26, "HIGH_TEMP")
    .when(col("humidity") > 65, "HIGH_HUMIDITY")
    .otherwise("NORMAL")
)
df_with_alerts.show()

# SQL Queries
# Register DataFrame as temporary view
df.createOrReplaceTempView("sensors")

print("\n=== SQL Query: Average temp by location ===")
sql_result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT location,
           COUNT(*) as total_readings,
           ROUND(AVG(temperature), 2) as avg_temp,
           ROUND(AVG(humidity), 2) as avg_humidity
    FROM sensors
    GROUP BY location
    ORDER BY avg_temp DESC
""")
sql_result.show()

# Window functions for time-series analysis
print("\n=== Running Average (Window Function) ===")
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

window_spec = Window.partitionBy("sensor_id").orderBy("timestamp").rowsBetween(-2, 0)
df_with_running_avg = df.withColumn(
    "running_avg_temp",
    avg("temperature").over(window_spec)
)
df_with_running_avg.show()

# Stop SparkSession
spark.stop()

# Time Complexity: O(n) for n rows
# Space Complexity: O(p) where p = number of partitions
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates Spark DataFrames and Spark SQL for processing structured IoT sensor data. DataFrames provide a higher-level API than RDDs with automatic schema inference and optimization through Spark SQL's Catalyst optimizer. The example shows filtering, aggregations, conditional columns using when(), SQL queries on registered temporary views, and window functions for time-series analysis.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n rows, O(k) for k groups in aggregation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(p) where p is the number of partitions in memory

examples.title: Python: Spark Streaming for Real-Time Data
examples.description: Demonstrates micro-batch stream processing for real-time IoT telemetry using DStreams
examples.code:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.streaming import StreamingContext
from pyspark.streaming.kafka import KafkaUtils
import json
import time

# Create SparkSession and StreamingContext
spark = SparkSession.builder\
    .appName("IoTStreamProcessing")\
    .config("spark.streaming.stopGracefullyOnShutdown", "true")\
    .getOrCreate()

# Batch interval: process every 5 seconds
ssc = StreamingContext(spark.sparkContext, batchDuration=5)

# Create DStream from TCP socket (for testing)
# In production, use Kafka: KafkaUtils.createDirectStream
lines = ssc.socketTextStream("localhost", 9999)

# Parse incoming JSON data
def parse_json(line):
    try:
        data = json.loads(line)
        return (data.get("sensor_id"), data.get("temperature"), 
                data.get("humidity"), data.get("timestamp"))
    except:
        return None

# Transform: Parse JSON and filter valid records
parsed_stream = lines.map(parse_json).filter(lambda x: x is not None)

# Windowed operations: Process data in sliding windows
# Window duration: 30 seconds, slide interval: 10 seconds
windowed_stream = parsed_stream.window(windowDuration=30, slideDuration=10)

# Count readings per sensor in each batch
def count_by_sensor(rdd):
    if rdd.isEmpty():
        return
    
    df = rdd.toDF(["sensor_id", "temperature", "humidity", "timestamp"])
    
    # Aggregate by sensor
    sensor_counts = df.groupBy("sensor_id").count()
    
    print("\n=== Sensor Readings Count ===")
    sensor_counts.show()
    
    # Calculate average temperature
    avg_temps = df.groupBy("sensor_id").agg({"temperature": "avg"})
    print("\n=== Average Temperature by Sensor ===")
    avg_temps.show()
    
    # Alert for high temperature
    high_temp = df.filter(df.temperature > 30)
    high_count = high_temp.count()
    if high_count > 0:
        print(f"ALERT: {high_count} readings with temperature > 30°C")

# Apply transformation to each RDD in the stream
parsed_stream.foreachRDD(count_by_sensor)

# Update state across batches (stateful streaming)
# Example: Running count of total readings per sensor
def update_function(new_values, running_count):
    if running_count is None:
        running_count = 0
    # Sum new values with existing count
    return sum(new_values) + running_count

# Create initial DStream with (sensor_id, 1) pairs
sensor_pairs = parsed_stream.map(lambda x: (x[0], 1))

# Update state with running totals
running_counts = sensor_pairs.updateStateByKey(update_function)

# Print running totals every batch
def print_state(rdd):
    if not rdd.isEmpty():
        print("\n=== Running Total Readings ===")
        for sensor, count in rdd.collect():
            print(f"{sensor}: {count}")

running_counts.foreachRDD(print_state)

# Start streaming
print("Starting Spark Streaming... Waiting for data on port 9999")
ssc.start()

# Wait for termination (press Ctrl+C to stop)
try:
    ssc.awaitTermination()
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\nStopping Spark Streaming...")
    ssc.stop(stopSparkContext=True, stopGraceFully=True)

# Time Complexity: O(batch_size) per micro-batch
# Space Complexity: O(window_size) for windowed operations
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates Spark Streaming for processing real-time IoT data. Spark Streaming divides the input stream into micro-batches (DStreams), where each batch is processed as an RDD. Key concepts shown include: windowed operations (sliding windows for aggregated analysis over time periods), stateful streaming (updateStateByKey for maintaining running totals across batches), and foreachRDD for applying custom processing to each micro-batch. In production, Kafka is typically used as the data source for fault-tolerant streaming.
examples.timeComplexity: O(b) per batch where b is batch size
examples.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is window size in batches
